summary: Self-healing wrapper: runs pipeline with automatic retry on partial failure
logs: [verd:f034.0] failed: API Error: [SSL: SSLV3_ALERT_BAD_RECORD_MAC] sslv3 alert bad record mac (_ssl.c:2546)
[review-pipeline] reachability check: 3 of 5 confirmed findings qualify

finding.reachability: Runs automatically for any authenticated admin on the troubleshooting settings page (via localizeSettings() at line 207). The DB row must first be poisoned — requires write access to the WBW Product Filter settings table (shop-admin level or a compromised WBW plugin).
finding.justification: unserialize($filter['setting_data']) is called on data fetched from a third-party plugin's database table with no allowed_classes restriction; a poisoned serialized payload in that table triggers object injection during every admin page load without any AJAX call.

detection_pattern.section_name: Object Injection: unserialize without allowed_classes
detection_pattern.description: Matches any call to unserialize() that does not specify the allowed_classes option. PHP unserialize without allowed_classes can trigger __wakeup/__destruct on arbitrary classes present in the codebase (POP chain), making it exploitable whenever the serialized input originates from DB rows, options, or transients written by users or other plugins.

verdict.reasoning:
The unserialize() call at line 710 of Troubleshooting.php is confirmed reachable with attacker-influenced data under specific but realistic conditions.

Full call chain: admin_init fires adminScripts() -> apply_filters('dgwt/wcas/scripts/admin/localize') -> Troubleshooting::localizeSettings() -> iterates getTests()['direct'] -> calls getTestProductFilterByWBW() via performTest() -> unserialize($filter['setting_data']).

This chain runs synchronously on every page load of the plugin settings page (?page=dgwt_wcas_settings), with no nonce and no explicit capability check in localizeSettings itself. WordPress restricts that URL to admin users by convention, but no programmatic check gates execution.

Sink analysis: Line 710: $raw = ( is_array($filter['setting_data']) ? $filter['setting_data'] : unserialize($filter['setting_data']) ). No allowed_classes argument is provided. The input is $filter['setting_data'] from \FrameWpf::_()->getModule('woofilters')->getModel()->getFromTbl(), which reads raw rows from the WBW Product Filter plugin's database table. The data is used without any sanitization, validation, or unserialize allowed_classes restriction.

Guards that must be satisfied (do NOT block exploitation, they are feature-presence checks): (1) WPF_VERSION constant must be defined — meaning WBW Product Filter must be active. (2) \FrameWpf class must exist. (3) getFromTbl() must return an array. (4) A row must have a 'setting_data' key. (5) setting_data must not already be an array (it is a serialized string in WBW's schema).

Attacker control path: An attacker who can write to the WBW Product Filter settings table (requires shop-admin privileges, a SQL injection in that plugin, or a compromised admin account) can store a malicious serialized POP chain in setting_data. On the next admin page load of the ajax-search plugin settings, unserialize() fires with that payload. No additional attacker interaction is needed after poisoning the DB.

Severity rationale: Rated medium rather than high because the prerequisite — write access to the WBW plugin's DB table — already implies near-admin-level compromise. The practical incremental risk is POP-chain execution (code execution, data exfiltration) beyond what a shop-admin can already do via the WordPress admin UI, but the attack path is realistic in multi-tenant or plugin-chaining scenarios (e.g., a second plugin with SQLi populates WBW's table). The finding is genuine and the code is exactly as described.

reachability.reasoning: The vulnerable unserialize() call at line 710 of Troubleshooting.php is reached only through: admin_init -> adminScripts() -> [inside isSettingsPage() guard] -> apply_filters('dgwt/wcas/scripts/admin/localize') -> Troubleshooting::localizeSettings() -> getTestProductFilterByWBW(). The decisive gate is in ajax-search-for-woocommerce.php at line 312: the entire localize block including the filter call is wrapped in `if ( \DgoraWcas\Helpers::isSettingsPage() )`. isSettingsPage() (Helpers.php line 538-543) requires both is_admin() (WordPress-enforced, only authenticated users with admin-panel access pass) AND $_GET['page'] === 'dgwt_wcas_settings'. WordPress's is_admin() combined with the admin-area URL means only users authenticated as admin or shop_manager can trigger this code path at all. An unauthenticated attacker is redirected to wp-login.php before admin_init fires for admin-specific contexts. A subscriber-level user cannot load ?page=dgwt_wcas_settings. There is no nonce on the localizeSettings execution path, but that is irrelevant — the gate is a WordPress session capability check. The full prerequisite chain requires (1) admin/shop_manager credentials to load the settings page AND (2) write access to the WBW Product Filter DB table to poison setting_data. Both prerequisites individually imply near-full-site-level access. No unauthenticated or low-privilege attacker has a path to the sink. The incremental risk over what an authenticated shop-admin can already do is marginal. Severity is downgraded to LOW.

finding.reachability: Any unauthenticated visitor on the internet; registered via wp_ajax_nopriv_ (and wc_ajax_ which is also public/unauthenticated). The phpcs:ignore comment at line 35 explicitly suppresses the nonce-check warning, confirming intentional omission.
finding.justification: getResultDetails() reads $_POST['items'] with no nonce or referer verification whatsoever; a cross-origin request can be forged to make any logged-in user's browser query product/taxonomy detail HTML on their behalf, leaking whatever canUserSee() would permit for that user's session — a read-only CSRF whose value is limited but not zero (e.g., probing whether a private product exists for an admin user).

detection_pattern.section_name: CSRF: public AJAX handler without nonce
detection_pattern.description: Finds wp_ajax_nopriv_ registrations (publicly callable AJAX handlers) and flags those whose corresponding callback does not contain a nonce verification call. Even read-only handlers can leak data via CSRF if the response differs by auth context.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed: getResultDetails() at line 29 has no nonce or referer check — the //phpcs:ignore WordPress.Security.NonceVerification.Missing comment at line 35 makes this explicit. The handler is registered via wc_ajax_ (not wp_ajax_nopriv_ as claimed — DGWT_WCAS_WC_AJAX_ENDPOINT is defined as true in the main plugin file at line 247, taking the line-17/18 branch). wc_ajax_ is equally public/unauthenticated (?wc-ajax=dgwt_wcas_result_details), so the finding's core reachability claim holds despite the wrong hook name. The canUserSee() method at Product.php:658-665 calls current_user_can('read_post') and current_user_can('edit_post'), meaning the response differs by auth context: a draft or hidden product returns empty HTML for an unauthenticated caller but returns full product details for an admin. This confirms the theoretical CSRF info-leak path (attacker's page POSTs to ?wc-ajax=dgwt_wcas_result_details with a private product ID; if an admin visits, the response leaks existence/details of that product). However, practical exploitability is substantially limited by: (1) modern browsers set SameSite=Lax on WordPress auth cookies by default, so cross-site POST requests do not carry the auth cookie — the response would be identical to an unauthenticated call; (2) even without SameSite, the response goes to the attacker's origin only if CORS allows it, which it does not by default; (3) the operation is read-only with no state mutation. The finding's severity=low is correct. The finding is real (code confirms absent nonce, confirmed auth-gated canUserSee, confirmed public endpoint), but the wp_ajax_nopriv_ attribution is factually wrong — it uses wc_ajax_ in the actual deployed code path.

finding.reachability: Reachable on any product search page when the woocommerce_get_filtered_term_product_counts_query filter fires (i.e., layered nav / attribute filters active). Same second-plugin precondition as the price-filter finding.
finding.justification: At line 153, the same pattern recurs in filter_counts_query(): `$query['where'] .= " AND $wpdb->posts.ID IN(" . implode( ',', $post_ids ) . ')'`. The $post_ids array comes from the untyped public filter dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids with no integer validation, allowing SQL injection if any hook returns non-integer elements.

detection_pattern.section_name: SQL Injection: unguarded concatenation into SQL WHERE clause via implode
detection_pattern.description: Catches direct string concatenation into a SQL where clause using implode() on an array, without integer sanitization. Common pattern in WooCommerce filter integration code where post ID arrays flow through WordPress hooks and are then embedded in raw SQL.

verdict.reasoning: The sink is real and unguarded: filter_counts_query() at line 153 concatenates $post_ids directly into a raw SQL WHERE clause via implode(',', $post_ids) with no intval/absint cast and no $wpdb->prepare(). The data path is: apply_filters('dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids', []) -> implode(',', ...) -> $query['where'] .= " AND ...". In the canonical (no-extra-plugin) flow, $post_ids is populated by Search.php::getProductIds(), which returns $postsIDsBuffer. That buffer is set from $postIn[] = $suggestion->ID where $suggestion->ID comes from get_posts() WP_Post->ID (a PHP integer from a DB-backed WordPress API call) -- so the canonical path is safe. However, the filter 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' is a public WordPress filter: any plugin/theme hooking it at priority > 10 can replace the array with arbitrary string values, which would flow directly into the raw SQL. Additionally, the intermediate filter 'dgwt/wcas/page_search_results/output' (Search.php line 271) lets a second plugin mutate $output['suggestions'] objects before their ->ID values are pushed into postsIDsBuffer, providing a second injection vector. There is no integer sanitization anywhere in either code path. The reachability requires a second-plugin precondition (consistent with the finding's stated reachability), making this a second-order vulnerability/API design flaw rather than a direct HTTP-to-SQL path. Severity medium is appropriate: exploitation requires admin-level plugin installation or a vulnerable co-installed plugin, but the SQL injection itself is unmitigated if that precondition is met. The finding's reachability description correctly acknowledges the second-plugin precondition.

reachability.reasoning: The vulnerable code path (filter_counts_query at line 153) is gated only by isProductSearchPage(), which checks three public GET parameters (?dgwt_wcas=1&post_type=product&s=...) — no nonce, no capability check, no authentication required. Any unauthenticated visitor can satisfy that page-level condition. However, the actual SQL injection requires a second plugin (or theme/mu-plugin) to hook 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' and return non-integer elements. In the canonical plugin-only flow, getProductIds() returns $postsIDsBuffer populated from WP_Post->ID values obtained via get_posts(), which are PHP integers from a DB-backed WordPress API — these are safe. The only path to inject non-integer values is through the public WordPress filter 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids', which any co-installed plugin can hook. Installing a plugin in WordPress requires manage_options / install_plugins capability — an administrator-level privilege. An unauthenticated or subscriber-level attacker cannot install plugins and therefore cannot place the hook that injects malicious values. The gate is not a nonce or cryptographic secret — it is the requirement for a second admin-installed plugin that itself contains or exposes a vulnerability. This is a second-order / API design flaw: the filter lacks integer sanitization at the consumption point, so any future or current co-plugin regression would flow directly into raw SQL with no mitigation. Because exploitation requires admin-level plugin installation, gate_breachable=false and severity is downgraded to LOW.

finding.reachability: Reachable on any product search page where the WOOF price filter widget is rendered. Exploitation requires a second plugin or theme hooking 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' and returning non-integer array elements, or a future regression in the upstream populator. The filter is public and documented.
finding.justification: At line 109, the output of apply_filters('dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids', []) is passed directly to implode(',', $post_ids) and concatenated into a raw SQL string: `$sql .= " AND $wpdb->posts.ID IN(" . implode( ',', $post_ids ) . ')'`. No integer cast (array_map('intval',...) or absint()) is applied, nor is $wpdb->prepare() used. Any WordPress hook registered on this public filter can inject non-integer elements (e.g., '1 UNION SELECT...') that break out of the IN() clause.

detection_pattern.section_name: SQL Injection: apply_filters array imploded into IN() clause without integer cast
detection_pattern.description: Catches PHP code that calls apply_filters() to obtain an array and immediately implodes it into a SQL fragment without integer sanitization. WordPress filters are public extension points; any plugin can alter the returned array. Without array_map('intval',...) or $wpdb->prepare(), SQL injection is possible via injected non-integer elements.

verdict.reasoning: The sink at WooCommerceProductsFilter.php line 109 is confirmed: apply_filters('dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids', []) output is directly imploded into a raw SQL IN() clause with no intval/absint cast and no $wpdb->prepare(). The canonical populator in Search.php::getProductIds() fills postsIDsBuffer from $post->ID values returned by get_posts(), which are integers from the database — so the baseline code path does not pass attacker-controlled strings. The vulnerability is second-order: the public WordPress filter 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' has no type enforcement, and any registered hook (another installed plugin, mu-plugin, or theme) can return non-integer array elements that flow directly into the SQL sink. No sanitizer, intval cast, or $wpdb->prepare() exists between the filter output and the SQL concatenation. The finding's reachability claim is accurate: exploitation requires a cooperating second component hooking the filter. Without such a component, an unauthenticated attacker has no direct path. The flaw is real and the severity 'medium' is appropriate — it represents a missing defense-in-depth control at a trust boundary, exploitable if any co-installed code (malicious or buggy) injects non-integer values.

reachability.gate_type: no gate — open handler (public GET parameters only; no nonce, no capability check, no secret)
reachability.reasoning: The code path to the SQL sink is gated only by is_search(), which checks for publicly controllable GET parameters (?dgwt_wcas=1&post_type=product&s=query) — any unauthenticated user can satisfy this check trivially. There is no nonce, no authentication cookie, and no capability check protecting the path. However, the SQL injection itself is NOT directly exploitable by an unauthenticated attacker: the filter 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' is populated by Search::getProductIds() (Search.php lines 949-954), which returns $this->postsIDsBuffer built from $post->ID values sourced from get_posts() — these are always integers from the database. An unauthenticated attacker cannot directly inject non-integer values into the filter output in a default WordPress install; doing so requires a second co-installed plugin, mu-plugin, or theme that hooks 'dgwt/wcas/search_page/result_post_ids' and returns attacker-controlled non-integer elements. No such hook exists in this plugin itself. Additionally, the vulnerable function only fires when the WOOF Products Filter plugin is also installed and active (it hooks 'woof_get_filtered_price_query', a WOOF-specific filter). The exploitation chain requires: (1) WOOF plugin active, (2) ajax-search-for-woocommerce active, (3) a third co-installed component hooking the public filter with non-integer values. Without a cooperating second component, an unauthenticated attacker has no direct path to inject into the SQL. The flaw represents a missing defense-in-depth control (no intval cast / no $wpdb->prepare()) at a public WordPress filter trust boundary, not a directly exploitable vulnerability in default installs. Severity is downgraded to LOW.

finding.reachability: Reachable by any unauthenticated user via wp_ajax_nopriv_prdctfltr_respond_550 (line 38); no authentication or capability check protects this handler.
finding.justification: The search phrase is taken directly from $_POST['pf_filters'][$_POST['pf_id']]['s'] (line 65) with no sanitization (no sanitize_text_field, wp_unslash, or strip_tags) before being passed to Helpers::searchProducts($phrase); safety depends entirely on downstream sanitization inside the search engine.

detection_pattern.section_name: Missing Input Sanitization: Raw $_POST nested key passed to search
detection_pattern.description: Detects places where a nested $_POST value used as a search phrase is not sanitized before use. Without upstream sanitization, security relies entirely on downstream callers — removing or changing those callers introduces injection risk.

verdict.reasoning: The code pattern exists exactly as described. Line 65 of set_search_post_ids_from_ajax() assigns $phrase = $_POST['pf_filters'][$_POST['pf_id']]['s'] with no upstream sanitize_text_field, wp_unslash, or strip_tags. The handler is reachable unauthenticated via wp_ajax_nopriv_prdctfltr_respond_550 (line 38) with no nonce or capability check (phpcs:disable at line 56 confirms intentional omission). However, downstream sanitization fully covers the value: Helpers::searchProducts($phrase) calls DGWT_WCAS()->nativeSearch->getSearchResults($phrase, true, 'product-ids'), and at Search.php line 175, because $return=true, the code unconditionally applies $keyword = sanitize_text_field($phrase) before any database query. The final result stored in $this->post_ids is integer IDs extracted by wp_list_pluck($results['suggestions'], 'ID'), so no XSS, SQLi, or other injection is possible through this path. The finding is real as a defense-in-depth / code quality concern (missing upstream sanitization that relies on downstream callers remaining correct), but the original 'medium' severity overstates the risk because exploitation requires circumventing downstream sanitization that is hardcoded in getSearchResults(). Severity is better classified as low/informational.

finding.reachability: Any authenticated WordPress user who can update their own profile display_name (subscriber and above by default) and whose display_name renders in the admin notice visible to other admins on the plugin settings page.
finding.justification: $current_user->display_name is concatenated directly into HTML markup without esc_html() or any escaping before being passed to printf(), so a user-controlled display_name containing HTML/script tags will be rendered verbatim in the admin notice.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: unescaped display_name in HTML output
detection_pattern.description: Catches concatenation or direct use of $current_user->display_name (or similar user-profile fields) into HTML strings without context-correct escaping. WordPress does not sanitize display_name for HTML on save, so it can contain tags that execute when echoed into page output.

verdict.reasoning: Three independent reasons make this finding false: 1. WRONG REACHABILITY — The notice is only rendered when shouldShowFeedbackNotice() returns true (NoticePolicy.php line 42), which requires current_user_can('install_plugins'). That capability is administrator-only. The finding's claim of 'subscriber and above' is incorrect. No subscriber-level user can trigger or see this notice. 2. SELF-XSS ONLY — $current_user->display_name is the display name of the same administrator who is currently viewing the notice. There is no mechanism by which a low-privileged user's display_name appears in this admin-facing notice. There is no cross-user attack vector. 3. WORDPRESS CORE SANITIZES display_name ON SAVE — WordPress core's wp_insert_user() / wp_update_user() in wp-includes/user.php passes display_name through sanitize_text_field(), which calls strip_tags() internally. HTML and script tags are stripped before being persisted to the database. An attacker cannot store '' in display_name through any normal WordPress user update API. The unescaped output at line 64 ('' . $current_user->display_name . '') is a code quality issue (missing esc_html()) but is not exploitable given: (a) admin-only gate via install_plugins capability check, (b) self-display of the current user's own name, and (c) WordPress-level HTML stripping on save. All three defenses must simultaneously fail for exploitation, and at least defenses (a) and (c) are robust and independent.
